section: Class 10 Economics - Chapter 4: Globalisation and the Indian Economy

concepts.lesson: Globalisation refers to the process of rapid integration or interconnection between countries through trade, investment, and technology. It leads to greater movement of goods, services, capital, and information across national boundaries.
concepts.real_example: A smartphone designed in the USA, assembled in China, and sold in India reflects globalisation.
key_points: Free flow of goods and services

concepts.lesson: MNCs are companies that own or control production in more than one country. They set up offices and factories abroad where labour and resources are cheaper to increase profits.
concepts.real_example: Samsung and Coca-Cola operate production units in multiple countries including India.
key_points: Operate in more than one country

concepts.lesson: Foreign trade involves exchange of goods and services between countries. Trade barriers such as tariffs are taxes imposed on imports to protect domestic industries. Reduction of trade barriers promotes globalisation.
concepts.real_example: Importing electronic goods from China into India is part of foreign trade.
key_points: Exports and imports form foreign trade
Lower trade barriers increase global trade

concepts.lesson: In 1991, India adopted liberalisation reforms to remove trade restrictions and open its economy to foreign investment. This led to increased competition, growth of industries, and expansion of markets.
concepts.real_example: After 1991, many foreign brands entered the Indian market.

concepts.lesson: The World Trade Organization (WTO) is an international body that sets rules for global trade. It aims to ensure fair trade practices but has been criticised for favoring developed countries.
concepts.real_example: WTO agreements regulate agricultural subsidies and trade policies.
key_points: Influences trade policies of member countries

concepts.lesson: Globalisation has benefited consumers through more choices and better quality goods. However, small producers and workers sometimes face challenges due to increased competition from large MNCs.
concepts.real_example: Small textile producers may struggle to compete with branded international products.
key_points: Growth of IT and service sector
